primary_diagnosis.name: Sick sinus syndrome, tachycardia-bradycardia variant, exacerbated by a recently commenced beta-blocker, haemodynamically symptomatic with syncope

pathophysiology_summary: Sick sinus syndrome is degeneration/fibrosis of the sinoatrial node and surrounding atrial tissue, common in the elderly, producing failure of impulse generation and conduction. The tachycardia-bradycardia (tachy-brady) variant alternates paroxysmal atrial tachyarrhythmias with inappropriate sinus bradycardia and prolonged sinus pauses; the abrupt post-tachycardia overdrive-suppression pauses cause cerebral hypoperfusion and syncope. Chronotropic incompetence (failure to raise heart rate with exertion) explains the months of profound fatigue and exertional breathlessness. A recently commenced beta-blocker (metoprolol), given for hypertension, blunts an already failing node — worsening the bradycardia and pauses and unmasking overt syncope. In this frail elder the immediate hazard is a prolonged sinus pause/asystole, recurrent syncope with traumatic falls, and sudden cardiac death. Definitive management is withdrawal of nodal-blocking agents and permanent pacemaker implantation after continuous rhythm monitoring; the diagnosis is confirmed by capturing the pauses on ECG/telemetry/Holter, which cannot be done remotely.

differential.evidence_basis: Alternating fast/slow heart rate with syncope in an elderly patient is the classic tachy-brady picture.
Common cause of syncope, but does not explain documented alternating tachy/brady extremes independent of a clear reflex trigger.
Lightheaded on standing fits, but does not explain the paroxysmal tachycardia or resting bradycardia.

differential.diagnosis: Acute myocardial infarction / acute coronary syndrome
differential.evidence_basis: Syncope in an elder mandates excluding ACS even without chest pain; kept live until an ECG/troponin is available.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, an unstable arrhythmia and ACS must both be entertained; a benign vasovagal label alone is premature.

differential.evidence_basis: Chronotropic incompetence over months + recent metoprolol + alternating rates + syncope converge on drug-worsened sinus node disease.
Recently commenced metoprolol can itself cause symptomatic bradycardia; here it is compounding underlying node disease rather than acting alone.
Another cause of bradycardic syncope; the history of paroxysmal atrial tachycardia points to sinus node rather than isolated AV node failure, but it cannot be excluded without an ECG.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The medication history is the pivot — eliciting the recent beta-blocker distinguishes a reversible iatrogenic contributor and is the key non-anchoring move.

differential.diagnosis: Sick sinus syndrome (tachy-brady), beta-blocker exacerbated, symptomatic with syncope
differential.evidence_basis: Syncope + resting HR 42 + alternating tachy/brady + chronotropic incompetence = unstable sinus node dysfunction requiring monitoring and pacing assessment.
No chest pain/diaphoresis lowers probability but ACS is only excluded with ECG and troponin, unavailable via telehealth.
Cannot account for documented bradycardia-tachycardia extremes and a resting HR of 42 unrelated to a reflex trigger.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is an unstable brady-arrhythmia with syncope; the correct action is escalation for continuous monitoring, not remote reassurance.

differential.diagnosis: Sick sinus syndrome (tachycardia-bradycardia variant), beta-blocker exacerbated
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed on hospital telemetry (prolonged sinus pauses); management is withdraw metoprolol and pace. Definitive rhythm capture is not achievable remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth ceiling is a presumptive unstable arrhythmia mandating urgent monitored transfer; the rhythm diagnosis is confirmed only in hospital.

red_flags.description: Unexplained syncope (loss of consciousness ~2 minutes) in a patient with a bradyarrhythmia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cardiac syncope from sinus pauses signals impending haemodynamic collapse; carries a risk of prolonged asystole and sudden cardiac death.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Recurrent syncope with traumatic injury, prolonged pause/asystole, sudden death if not monitored and paced.

red_flags.description: Profound symptomatic resting bradycardia (HR ~42) with pallor, drowsiness and weakness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Symptomatic bradycardia indicates critical failure of the primary pacemaker and low cardiac output.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to prolonged pause/asystole and cardiac arrest.

red_flags.description: Alternating paroxysmal tachycardia and severe bradycardia (tachy-brady) documented by staff over 48 hours
red_flags.clinical_significance: Hallmark of sinus node dysfunction; the post-tachycardia overdrive-suppression pauses are the mechanism of syncope.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Misattribution to a benign cause and failure to arrange continuous monitoring.

red_flags.description: Recently commenced beta-blocker (metoprolol) blunting an already failing sinus node
red_flags.clinical_significance: A reversible/iatrogenic contributor — nodal-blocking agents must be withheld immediately; missing it delays the single easiest corrective step.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued dosing deepens the bradycardia and pauses.

red_flags.description: Frail elder now unable to stand, high falls risk with unpredictable haemodynamic shifts
red_flags.clinical_significance: Recurrent syncope in a frail elder risks fractures and head injury; supine rest and supervision are protective until transfer.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fall with fracture or intracranial injury during a bradycardic/syncopal episode.

red_flags.description: Cardiac syncope in an elder — acute coronary syndrome not yet excluded
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absence of chest pain does not exclude ACS in the elderly; ECG and troponin are required, and cannot be obtained via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed silent ACS presenting as syncope/arrhythmia.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling the syncope as a benign faint or orthostatic drop and under-escalating, ignoring the documented resting bradycardia and paroxysmal tachycardia that a reflex faint cannot explain.
Failing to elicit the recently commenced metoprolol and therefore not advising it be withheld — the single most reversible contributor.
Attributing months of fatigue and today's collapse to ageing/deconditioning and missing chronotropic incompetence from sinus node disease.
Trying to 'confirm' or exclude the arrhythmia over chat; the rhythm and pauses can only be captured on ECG/telemetry, so the safe move is escalation, not remote diagnosis.
Reaching for a rate-controlling AV-nodal blocker (further beta-blocker, verapamil/diltiazem, digoxin) for the fast spells — this deepens the bradycardia and can precipitate asystole.

risk_scores.clinical_implication: Symptomatic bradyarrhythmia with syncope warrants urgent monitored transfer and pacing assessment.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG and the underlying rhythm / SA exit block
Continuous telemetry to capture sinus pauses and their duration
Gold-standard blood pressure and true beat-to-beat heart rate
Troponin and electrolytes to exclude ACS and reversible metabolic causes
Confirmation that there was no occult head injury from the fall
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise an unstable brady-arrhythmia with syncope; call 000 for an emergency ambulance with cardiac monitoring and urgent transfer to ED. Instruct nursing staff to withhold metoprolol and any other heart-slowing medication now, keep the patient lying flat, monitor pulse and breathing, and be ready to start CPR if she becomes unresponsive and pulseless.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The danger is under-action: labelling this a vasovagal faint or dehydration, reassuring, and deferring to routine review instead of arranging monitored transfer.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ESC 2021 Guidelines on cardiac pacing and cardiac resynchronization therapy
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Symptomatic sinus node dysfunction with syncope is an indication for permanent pacing after excluding reversible causes such as rate-slowing drugs.
